standard.meaning: the terrace / the outdoor seating area
standard.notes: In France, café terraces are an iconic part of daily life. 'En terrasse' = outside on the terrace. 'On s'assoit en terrasse ?' = Shall we sit outside?
examples: On s'assoit en terrasse ?
Shall we sit on the terrace?
La terrasse est pleine aujourd'hui.
The terrace is full today.

standard.notes: 'Une tasse de café' = a cup of coffee. 'Une tasse de thé' = a cup of tea. Different from 'un verre' (a glass) which is used for cold drinks.
examples: Je voudrais une tasse de thé, s'il vous plaît.
I would like a cup of tea, please.
La tasse est chaude, fais attention !
The cup is hot, be careful!

standard.notes: 'Le thé vert' = green tea. 'Le thé noir' = black tea. 'Un thé à la menthe' = mint tea (very popular in North African French culture). Tea is less common than coffee in France but gaining popularity.
examples: Tu préfères le thé ou le café ?
Do you prefer tea or coffee?
Un thé à la menthe, s'il vous plaît.
A mint tea, please.

standard.notes: 'Un morceau de sucre' = a sugar cube (the traditional French way). 'Avec ou sans sucre ?' = With or without sugar? 'Sucré' = sweet (adjective).
examples: Vous voulez du sucre ?
Do you want some sugar?
Je prends mon café sans sucre.
I take my coffee without sugar.

standard.notes: In a café or restaurant, 'la carte' is the menu. It can also mean 'the card' or 'the map' in other contexts. 'La carte des boissons' = the drinks menu. 'À la carte' = choosing individual items (not a set menu).
examples: Est-ce que je peux voir la carte ?
Can I see the menu?
Il y a beaucoup de choix sur la carte.
There are many choices on the menu.

advanced.meaning: the terrace / the outdoor seating area
advanced.notes: In France, café terraces are an iconic part of daily life. 'En terrasse' = outside on the terrace. 'On s'assoit en terrasse ?' = Shall we sit outside?
examples: On s'assoit en terrasse ?
Shall we sit on the terrace?
La terrasse est pleine aujourd'hui.
The terrace is full today.

examples: Je voudrais une tasse de thé, s'il vous plaît.
I would like a cup of tea, please.
La tasse est chaude, fais attention !
The cup is hot, be careful!

advanced.notes: 'Le thé vert' = green tea. 'Le thé noir' = black tea. 'Un thé à la menthe' = mint tea (very popular in North African French culture). Tea is less common than coffee in France but gaining popularity.
examples: Tu préfères le thé ou le café ?
Do you prefer tea or coffee?
Un thé à la menthe, s'il vous plaît.
A mint tea, please.

advanced.notes: 'Un morceau de sucre' = a sugar cube (the traditional French way). 'Avec ou sans sucre ?' = With or without sugar? 'Sucré' = sweet (adjective).
examples: Vous voulez du sucre ?
Do you want some sugar?
Je prends mon café sans sucre.
I take my coffee without sugar.

advanced.notes: In a café or restaurant, 'la carte' is the menu. It can also mean 'the card' or 'the map' in other contexts. 'La carte des boissons' = the drinks menu. 'À la carte' = choosing individual items (not a set menu).
examples: Est-ce que je peux voir la carte ?
Can I see the menu?
Il y a beaucoup de choix sur la carte.
There are many choices on the menu.

advanced.notes: 'Les boissons chaudes' = hot drinks. 'Les boissons fraîches' = cold drinks. 'La carte des boissons' = the drinks menu. From the verb 'boire' (to drink).
examples: Quelle boisson voulez-vous ?
What drink would you like?
Les boissons chaudes sont à deux euros.
Hot drinks are two euros.

advanced.notes: Very popular in French cafés. 'Un sirop de menthe' = mint syrup (served with water, a classic French drink). 'Un sirop de grenadine' = grenadine syrup. 'Un diabolo menthe' = lemonade with mint syrup.
examples: Un sirop de menthe à l'eau, s'il vous plaît.
A mint syrup with water, please.
Les enfants aiment le sirop de grenadine.
Children like grenadine syrup.

dialogue: Il fait beau aujourd'hui. On va au café ?
Bonne idée ! On s'assoit en terrasse ?
Oui, j'y vais souvent. La terrasse est très agréable.
Je vais prendre un thé. Et toi ?
Moi, un café. Est-ce que je peux voir la carte ?
Tiens, la voilà. Tu veux du sucre avec ton café ?
Non, je prends mon café sans sucre. Merci !

questions.question: Where do they decide to sit?

standard.summary: Two friends decide to go to a café on a nice day. They sit on the terrace, which speaker A often visits. Speaker A orders a tea, while speaker B orders a coffee without sugar. Speaker B asks to see the menu.

dialogue: On se retrouve au café de la Place Victor Hugo ?
D'accord ! J'y suis déjà. Je suis en terrasse.
Super, j'y arrive dans cinq minutes.
Prends ton temps. Je regarde la carte en attendant.
Salut ! Ah, il y a beaucoup de boissons sur la carte.
Oui ! Moi, je vais prendre un sirop de menthe à l'eau. J'y viens souvent et c'est ma boisson préférée ici.
Intéressant. Moi, je voudrais un thé vert. Avec du sucre.
Tu mets toujours du sucre dans ton thé ?
Oui, j'y mets toujours un morceau. C'est meilleur comme ça !
Moi, je n'y mets jamais de sucre. Mais chacun ses goûts !

questions.question: How does speaker A take their tea?
What does 'j'y viens souvent' mean in context?

advanced.summary: Two friends meet at a café on Place Victor Hugo. Speaker B is already sitting on the terrace and looking at the menu. Speaker B orders a mint syrup with water — their favourite drink at this café. Speaker A orders a green tea with one sugar cube. They playfully disagree about whether to put sugar in tea.

standard.title: The Pronoun Y (Replacing à + Place)
standard.explanation:
The pronoun **y** replaces a **place** that was already mentioned. It means **'there'** or **'to there'**.

**When to use y:**
When you would repeat **à + a place** (or another preposition of place like 'dans', 'en', 'chez').

**Where y goes:** Before the verb (or before the auxiliary in passé composé).

**Examples:**
- Tu vas **au café** ? → Oui, j'**y** vais. (Are you going to the café? → Yes, I'm going there.)
- Elle est **à la maison** ? → Oui, elle **y** est. (Is she at home? → Yes, she's there.)
- Vous allez **en France** ? → Oui, nous **y** allons. (Are you going to France? → Yes, we're going there.)

**With negation:**
- Je n'**y** vais pas. (I'm not going there.)
- Il n'**y** est pas. (He's not there.)

**Common fixed expressions:**
- **On y va !** = Let's go! (literally: we're going there!)
- **J'y suis !** = I've got it! / I'm there!
- **Il y a** = there is/are (you already know this one!)

examples.french: Tu vas au parc ? — Oui, j'y vais.
examples.english: Are you going to the park? — Yes, I'm going there.

examples.french: Elle habite à Lyon ? — Oui, elle y habite.
examples.english: Does she live in Lyon? — Yes, she lives there.

drills.question: Complete: Tu vas au café ? — Oui, j'___ vais. (Yes, I'm going there.)
drills.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'au café' (the place). It goes before the verb: j'y vais.

drills.question: What does 'J'y suis' mean?
drills.explanation: 'Y' replaces the place. 'J'y suis' = I am there.

drills.question: Complete: Elle va à la bibliothèque ? — Non, elle n'___ va pas. (No, she's not going there.)
drills.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'à la bibliothèque'. In negation: n'y va pas.

drills.question: How do you say 'Let's go!' using the pronoun y?
drills.explanation: 'Y' goes before the verb: On y va ! This is a very common expression.

drills.question: Complete: Vous habitez en France ? — Oui, nous ___ habitons. (Yes, we live there.)
drills.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'en France'. Nous y habitons = We live there.

advanced.title: The Pronoun Y — Place Replacement, Position & Common Expressions
advanced.explanation:
**What 'y' replaces:**
'Y' replaces a prepositional phrase indicating a **place** — most commonly **à + place**, but also **dans**, **en**, **sur**, **chez**, etc.:
- Tu vas **au café** ? → Tu **y** vas ? (Are you going there?)
- Il est **dans sa chambre** ? → Il **y** est ? (Is he there?)
- Vous allez **chez le médecin** ? → Vous **y** allez ? (Are you going there?)

**Position of y:**
| Tense | Position | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Present | Before verb | J'**y** vais. |
| Passé composé | Before auxiliary | J'**y** suis allé. |
| Negation | ne + **y** + verb + pas | Je n'**y** vais pas. |
| Near future | Before infinitive | Je vais **y** aller. |
| Imperative (affirm.) | After verb + hyphen | Vas-**y** ! (Go!) |
| Imperative (neg.) | Before verb | N'**y** va pas ! (Don't go!) |

**Common expressions with y:**
- **On y va !** = Let's go!
- **Vas-y !** = Go ahead! / Go for it!
- **Allons-y !** = Let's go!
- **J'y suis, j'y reste.** = I'm here and I'm staying. (a famous quote)
- **Ça y est !** = That's it! / It's done!
- **Il y a** = there is/are

**Important:** 'Y' does NOT replace people. For people, use indirect object pronouns (lui, leur).

examples.english: I'm going to go there tomorrow.

drills.question: Complete: Tu es allé au café ? — Oui, j'___ suis allé. (Yes, I went there.)
drills.explanation: In passé composé, 'y' goes before the auxiliary (être/avoir): j'y suis allé.

drills.question: What does 'Vas-y !' mean?
drills.explanation: 'Vas-y' in the imperative is a common expression meaning 'go ahead' or 'go for it'.

drills.question: Complete: Elle va aller à Paris ? — Oui, elle va ___ aller. (Yes, she's going to go there.)
drills.explanation: In near future (aller + infinitive), 'y' goes before the infinitive: va y aller.

drills.question: Where does 'y' go in a negative sentence?
drills.explanation: Negation: ne + y + verb + pas. Example: Je n'y vais pas.

drills.question: Complete: Nous n'___ sommes pas allés. (We didn't go there.)
drills.explanation: In negative passé composé: ne + y + auxiliary + pas + participle.

drills.question: What does 'Ça y est !' mean?
drills.explanation: 'Ça y est' is a fixed expression meaning 'That's it!' or 'It's done!'

drills.question: Rewrite using y: Tu habites à Marseille ? — Oui, j'___ habite. (Yes, I live there.)
drills.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'à Marseille': j'y habite = I live there.

drills.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'y' in the imperative?
drills.explanation: In the affirmative imperative with 'tu', add -s to 'va' before 'y' and use a hyphen: Vas-y !

standard.question: What does 'la terrasse' mean in a café context?
standard.explanation: 'La terrasse' is the outdoor seating area of a café. 'S'asseoir en terrasse' = to sit outside.

standard.question: Fill in the blank: Tu vas au café ? — Oui, j'___ vais. (Yes, I'm going there.)
standard.explanation: 'Y' replaces the place (au café). It goes before the verb.

standard.question: How do you say 'a cup of tea' in French?
standard.explanation: 'Une tasse de thé' = a cup of tea. 'Un verre' is for glasses (cold drinks).

standard.question: Fill in the blank: Je prends mon café sans ___. (I take my coffee without sugar.)
standard.explanation: 'Sans sucre' = without sugar. 'Avec sucre' = with sugar.

standard.question: What does 'la carte' mean in a café?
standard.explanation: In a café or restaurant, 'la carte' means the menu.

advanced.question: What does 'la boisson' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'La boisson' means the drink or beverage. From 'boire' (to drink).

advanced.question: Fill in the blank: Tu es allé au café ? — Oui, j'___ suis allé hier. (Yes, I went there yesterday.)
advanced.explanation: In passé composé, 'y' goes before the auxiliary: j'y suis allé.

advanced.question: What is 'un sirop de menthe' ?
advanced.explanation: 'Le sirop de menthe' is mint syrup, typically served with water. A classic French café drink.

advanced.question: Fill in the blank: Elle va aller à Paris ? — Oui, elle va ___ aller demain. (Yes, she's going to go there tomorrow.)
advanced.explanation: In near future (aller + infinitive), 'y' goes before the infinitive: va y aller.

advanced.question: What does 'Vas-y !' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Vas-y' is a common imperative expression meaning 'Go ahead!' or 'Go for it!'

advanced.question: Fill in the blank: Je n'___ vais pas souvent. (I don't go there often.)
advanced.explanation: In negation, 'y' goes between 'ne' and the verb: n'y vais pas.

standard.scenario: You are meeting a friend at a French café. Greet them, suggest sitting on the terrace, look at the menu together, and order your drinks. Ask your friend what they would like.
keyPhrases: On s'assoit en terrasse ? (Shall we sit on the terrace?)
Je voudrais un(e)… s'il vous plaît. (I would like a… please.)
Une tasse de thé / café (A cup of tea / coffee)
Avec ou sans sucre ? (With or without sugar?)
standard.modelAnswer: Salut ! Ça fait plaisir de te voir. On s'assoit en terrasse ? Il fait beau aujourd'hui. Est-ce que je peux voir la carte ? Moi, je voudrais une tasse de thé avec du sucre, s'il vous plaît. Et toi, qu'est-ce que tu veux ?
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The model answer uses café vocabulary naturally (terrasse, carte, tasse, thé, sucre), polite ordering phrases (je voudrais, s'il vous plaît), and engages the friend with questions.
standard.tip: In French cafés, you can use 'je voudrais' (I would like) or 'je vais prendre' (I'll have) to order politely.

advanced.scenario: You are a regular at a French café and are showing it to a friend who has never been there. Describe the café, recommend your favourite drink, order for both of you, and use the pronoun 'y' naturally when talking about the café.
keyPhrases: J'y viens souvent. (I come here often.)
On y va ! / Vas-y, choisis ! (Let's go! / Go ahead, choose!)
La carte des boissons (The drinks menu)
Je te recommande le… (I recommend the…)
advanced.modelAnswer: Bienvenue ! J'y viens tous les samedis — c'est mon café préféré. On s'assoit en terrasse, il y a de la place. Tiens, voilà la carte des boissons. Je te recommande le sirop de menthe à l'eau, c'est délicieux. Moi, j'y prends toujours un thé vert sans sucre. Vas-y, choisis ta boisson ! Tu veux une tasse de thé aussi, ou tu préfères autre chose ?
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'y' three times naturally (j'y viens, il y a, j'y prends), recommends a drink, orders confidently, and uses café vocabulary (terrasse, carte, boissons, sirop, thé, tasse, sucre).
